disclaimer: Refreshed by the scheduled research agent against live sources (AAA, EIA, Trading Economics, World Bank/IMF, Reuters/CNBC). Contextual figures here may lag the live TradingView and OilPrice.com quotes shown above. Baselines labelled 'pre-war' are fixed reference points (late Feb 2026) and do not change.

hormuz.insuranceStatus: War-risk premium ~0.2-0.4% of hull value (from 0.125% pre-crisis), ~+$250k per large-tanker transit
hormuz.summary: The Strait of Hormuz — the chokepoint for roughly a fifth of seaborne crude — has been effectively closed since early March under a 'dual blockade': Iran controls passage through a toll regime while the US Navy interdicts Iran-linked traffic. Transits have collapsed from ~140/day to a trickle (about 191 vessels crossed in all of April per Kpler), removing roughly 10 million barrels/day of supply from the seaborne market.

timeline.event: US launches Operation Epic Fury against Iranian military and naval targets; Iran retaliates and first merchant ships are attacked.
Iran declares the Strait closed; Lloyd's underwriters pull standard war-risk cover for Gulf transits.
IRGC announces the Strait is shut to any vessel travelling to/from the US, Israel and their allies; Brent peaks near $118/bbl on the quarter.
Conditional ceasefire agreed; Iran begins charging $1M+ tolls per transit as a handful of tankers move.
US Navy begins enforcing a blockade of Iran-linked port traffic; the IMO reports ~20,000 mariners and ~2,000 ships stranded in the Gulf by April 21.

priceHistory.label: Nears $4.00 — highest in real terms in 2+ years
Easing as SPR barrels reach market

commodities.name: Wheat
commodities.detail: CBOT July futures ~580c/bu; easing on favorable US growing conditions despite Gulf-route risk.

commodities.name: Corn
commodities.detail: Near a seven-week low as favorable Midwest weather lifts harvest expectations.

commodities.name: Soybeans
commodities.detail: Held near a five-week low (~$11.7/bu) on strong US planting progress.

commodities.name: Rice
commodities.detail: Down from ten-month highs; ample global inventories led by record Indian output offset Gulf-route disruption.

commodities.name: Cooking oil
commodities.detail: Vegetable-oil index elevated on biofuel demand and higher freight on key routes.

commodities.name: Coffee
commodities.detail: Arabica near its lowest since Nov 2024 on a record Brazil harvest, but still well above pre-war.

keyPlayers.warImpact: World's largest producer at a record ~13.5 Mbpd. DOE has tapped the SPR (down to ~384M bbl from 411M at end-2025) via a ~172M-bbl exchange to cushion summer pump prices; domestic output unaffected but refiners face higher input costs.
Key OPEC swing producer with ~2.5 Mbpd spare capacity, but Hormuz blocks most Gulf exports; rerouting limited volumes via the East-West pipeline to the Red Sea.
Largely unaffected and benefiting from higher prices; expanding flows to China and India.
Under active strikes; exports (mostly to China) curtailed and Kharg Island terminal status uncertain. Economy forecast to contract ~10%.
Southern (Basra) output down more than 70%; Gulf-facing exports severely disrupted, Kirkuk-Ceyhan at reduced capacity.

consumerImpact.note: How the oil shock is reaching households at the pump and beyond. Baselines are late-February 2026; current prices have eased from the late-April/early-May peak as SPR barrels reach the market.

fuels.source: Wikipedia — Economic impact of the 2026 Iran war

keyFacts.fact: Brent crude ended Q1 2026 at $118/bbl after starting the year near $61 — the largest quarterly price increase on an inflation-adjusted basis in EIA data going back to 1988. It has since eased to roughly $93/bbl.
The DOE has released about 17.5 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve since March under a ~172M-bbl exchange program, with repayment-in-kind plus an 18-24% premium due in 2026-2029.

The World Bank projects global energy prices will rise about 24% in 2026 to their highest level since 2022, and warns up to 45 million more people could face acute food insecurity if the conflict is prolonged.
The IEA characterized the Hormuz disruption as the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market, with an initial loss of roughly 10 million barrels/day of supply.
keyFacts.source: Wikipedia — Economic impact of the 2026 Iran war
